instruments: National Bank Act, 12 U.S.C. 21 et seq., and 12 CFR 5.20 (OCC chartering regulation for national banks and national trust banks), as amended by a final rule published in the Federal Register 2 March 2026 (Doc. 2026-04088, 91 FR 9977), effective 1 April 2026 -- the general chartering authority under which OCC crypto-related bank and trust-bank charters are granted; not a crypto-specific statute
OCC News Release 2025-125, 'OCC Announces Conditional Approvals for Five National Trust Bank Charter Applications' (12 December 2025), with attached corporate decision documents

OCC: Digital Assets Licensing Applications page (occ.gov), listing pending national bank/national trust bank charter and conversion applications from entities offering digital-asset products or services

Federal Reserve Board press release, 'Federal Reserve Board announces the withdrawal of guidance for banks related to their crypto-asset and dollar token activities' (24 April 2025), rescinding SR 22-6/CA 22-6 and SR 23-8/CA 23-5, and jointly withdrawing (with the FDIC and OCC) two 2023 interagency statements on crypto-asset risks (3 January 2023) and crypto-asset-related liquidity risks (23 February 2023)

Federal Reserve Board press release, 'Federal Reserve Board announces it will sunset its novel activities supervision program' (15 August 2025), rescinding the 2023 supervisory letter (SR 23-7) that established the program

Federal Reserve Board press release, 'Federal Reserve Board withdraws 2023 policy statement and issues new policy statement' on Section 9(13) of the Federal Reserve Act (17 December 2025), and the resulting Policy Statement on Section 9(13) of the Federal Reserve Act, published in the Federal Register 22 December 2025 (Doc. 2025-23548, 90 FR 59731), effective 22 December 2025
Executive Order 14178, 'Strengthening American Leadership in Digital Financial Technology' (23 January 2025), Section 5 (central bank digital currency prohibition)

Executive Order 14405, 'Integrating Financial Technology Innovation into Regulatory Frameworks' (19 May 2026), Section 4 (Federal Reserve payment-account-access evaluation directive), published in the Federal Register 22 May 2026 (Doc. 2026-10399)

Federal Reserve Board press release, 'Federal Reserve Board requests public comment on a proposal to establish a "payment account"' (20 May 2026), and the resulting Notice, 'Proposed Revisions to the Federal Reserve Policy on Payment System Risk and the Guidelines for Evaluating Account and Services Requests,' published in the Federal Register 26 May 2026 (Doc. 2026-10375), comment period closing 60 days after publication (approximately 25 July 2026) -- a proposal, not yet in force

H.R. 1919, Anti-CBDC Surveillance State Act, 119th Congress -- passed by the House of Representatives 17 July 2025 (Roll Call 201, 219-210); Anti-CBDC provisions incorporated into a House amendment to S. 1318 and recorded at the Senate desk 29 April 2026 (not enacted)

GENIUS Act, Public Law 119-27 (enacted 18 July 2025) -- Federal Reserve's role as primary federal regulator for state-member-bank-subsidiary permitted payment stablecoin issuers is a statutory fact of this Act; full treatment is in the Stablecoins pillar and cross-referenced here rather than duplicated

OCC Interpretive Letters #1170 (22 July 2020), #1183 (7 March 2025), and #1184 (7 May 2025) on national bank crypto-asset custody, stablecoin-reserve, and node-verification/DLT activities -- full treatment is in the Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillar and cross-referenced here rather than duplicated
standing_summary: There is no dedicated federal banking-and-money statute for crypto assets; this pillar covers how the OCC and the Federal Reserve apply their existing, general chartering, supervisory, and payment-system authorities to bank and bank-adjacent participants in digital-asset activity, and that application-of-general-law posture -- not a purpose-built regime -- is the structural fact here. OCC: the OCC charters national banks and national trust banks under the National Bank Act and 12 CFR 5.20, and has used that general authority to charter a wave of crypto-native applicants since December 2025. In OCC News Release 2025-125 (12 December 2025), the OCC announced conditional approval of five national trust bank charter applications: two de novo charters, for First National Digital Currency Bank and Ripple National Trust Bank, and three conversions from state trust company to national trust bank, for BitGo Bank & Trust, National Association; Fidelity Digital Assets, National Association; and Paxos Trust Company, National Association. Comptroller of the Currency Jonathan V. Gould was quoted stating that 'new entrants into the federal banking sector are good for consumers, the banking industry and the economy,' and the release states the OCC 'applied the same rigorous review and standards it applies to all charter applications.' Separately, Circle Internet Group's own 10 July 2026 announcement states that the OCC granted final approval permitting First National Digital Currency Bank, N.A. (to operate as Circle National Trust) to open on or after that date; this research corroborates the underlying December 2025 conditional approval directly against the OCC's own release but could not locate a distinct OCC-issued press release specifically confirming the July 2026 final-approval date, and flags that in open items. As of this research, the OCC's own Digital Assets Licensing Applications page lists twelve further pending national bank/national trust bank applications from digital-asset entities, each shown only as 'pending' with no OCC characterization of likely outcome: CBW Bank, Catena Trust Bank N.A., Payward National Trust Company, Agora National Trust Bank, OpenReserve Bank N.A., Lorum National Trust Bank N.A., Bastion Platforms National Trust Company, EDX Trust N.A., Revolut Bank US N.A., zerohash national trust bank, PAYO Digital Bank N.A., and World Liberty Trust Company N.A. On 2 March 2026 the OCC published a final rule (effective 1 April 2026) amending 12 CFR 5.20 to replace the phrase 'fiduciary activities' with 'the operations of a trust company and activities related thereto' in describing national trust banks' permissible activities, which the OCC frames as clarifying -- not expanding -- the longstanding scope of national trust bank authority to include non-fiduciary activities such as custody. The OCC's operative interpretive-letter line permitting national banks to provide crypto-asset custody, hold stablecoin reserves, and participate in independent node verification networks (Interpretive Letters #1170, #1183, and #1184) is tracked in full in the Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillar and is cross-referenced, not re-detailed, here. Federal Reserve -- supervisory guidance: on 24 April 2025 the Federal Reserve Board rescinded its 2022 supervisory letter (SR 22-6/CA 22-6) that had established an expectation that state member banks provide advance notification of planned or current crypto-asset activities, and its 2023 supervisory letter (SR 23-8/CA 23-5) establishing a supervisory nonobjection process for state member bank dollar-token activities; the Board's own release states 'the Board will no longer expect banks to provide notification and will instead monitor banks' crypto-asset activities through the normal supervisory process.' The same release states the Board joined the FDIC and OCC in withdrawing from two 2023 interagency statements -- one on crypto-asset risks (3 January 2023) and one on crypto-asset-related liquidity risks (23 February 2023) -- with the OCC having already withdrawn from both on 7 March 2025 in connection with Interpretive Letter #1183. On 15 August 2025, the Board announced it would sunset its novel activities supervision program, a dedicated supervisory program for crypto and fintech activities established by a 2023 supervisory letter (SR 23-7), stating that since establishing the program the Board has 'strengthened its understanding of those activities, related risks, and bank risk management practices,' and that supervision of novel activities will be reintegrated into the normal supervisory process rather than run through a separate program. On 17 December 2025 (Federal Register publication 22 December 2025), the Board withdrew its 2023 policy statement interpreting Section 9(13) of the Federal Reserve Act -- which had limited state member banks to activities permissible for banks supervised by other federal banking regulators -- and issued a replacement policy statement. The new policy statement states the Board's general view that 'the same activity, presenting the same risks, should be subject to the same regulatory framework,' and the Board's own release states the new statement 'creates an avenue for both insured and uninsured Board-supervised state member banks to engage in certain innovative activities.' Federal Reserve -- payment-system policy and master account access: on 19 May 2026, Executive Order 14405, 'Integrating Financial Technology Innovation into Regulatory Frameworks,' directed the Federal Reserve Board to 'conduct a comprehensive evaluation of the legal, regulatory, and policy framework governing access to Reserve Bank payment accounts and payment services' by uninsured depository institutions and non-bank financial companies, including firms engaged in digital-asset activities, within 120 days, and, if existing law is found to permit expanded access, to establish transparent application procedures with a 90-day decision timeline. On 20 May 2026 the Board requested public comment on a proposal to establish a 'payment account,' which the Board's own release describes as available to 'legally eligible financial institutions' for 'the specific purpose of clearing and settling their payments'; payment account holders 'would not have access to intraday credit or the discount window' and 'would not earn interest on balances held at a Reserve Bank,' with closing-balance limits tied to expected payment activity, and the release states the proposal 'would not expand or otherwise change legal eligibility for access to accounts or payment services from the Federal Reserve' -- i.e., it is framed as a new account type within existing eligibility law, not an eligibility expansion. The proposal, published in the Federal Register 26 May 2026, has a comment period closing 60 days after publication (approximately 25 July 2026, after this pillar state's generation date); it is a proposal only and is not yet in force. The Federal Reserve's role as the primary federal regulator for GENIUS Act permitted payment stablecoin issuers that are state-member-bank subsidiaries is a statutory fact of the GENIUS Act, tracked in full in the Stablecoins pillar and cross-referenced, not duplicated, here. Central bank digital currency: the officially stated federal position is set by Executive Order 14178, 'Strengthening American Leadership in Digital Financial Technology' (23 January 2025), Section 5 of which states that 'agencies are hereby prohibited from undertaking any action to establish, issue, or promote' a CBDC 'within the jurisdiction of the United States or abroad,' and that any ongoing agency CBDC-related plans or initiatives 'shall be immediately terminated.' This is an executive order, not a statute. Separately, H.R. 1919, the Anti-CBDC Surveillance State Act, which would statutorily prohibit Federal Reserve banks from offering products or maintaining accounts for individuals or issuing a CBDC, passed the House of Representatives on 17 July 2025 (Roll Call 201, 219-210); its Anti-CBDC provisions were incorporated into a House amendment to S. 1318 and recorded at the Senate desk on 29 April 2026, but no Senate agreement to that amendment had been identified as of the most recent sources located for this research, and the bill is not enacted. This pillar accordingly records that the only currently binding federal constraint on a US CBDC is an executive order, not permanent legislation, and makes no prediction about whether, or when, that could change. This pillar covers the federal level only: state banking charters, including special-purpose depository institution regimes, are out of scope and are not tracked here.

open_items: Circle Internet Group's own 10 July 2026 announcement states the OCC granted final approval for First National Digital Currency Bank, N.A. (Circle National Trust) to open on or after that date. This research corroborated the underlying 12 December 2025 conditional approval directly against the OCC's own news release, but did not locate a distinct OCC-issued press release confirming the specific July 2026 final-approval/authorized-to-open date; that fact is reported here as stated by the regulated entity, not independently confirmed against a primary OCC release, and should be re-checked against occ.gov before being treated as fully verified.

The twelve pending applications listed on the OCC's Digital Assets Licensing Applications page are a snapshot as of this research's access date; charter application status at the OCC has been changing rapidly (multiple approvals and new filings within weeks of each other across late 2025 and 2026) and this list should not be treated as static.

OCC Interpretive Letters #1170, #1183, and #1184 (crypto-asset custody, stablecoin-reserve, and node-verification/DLT activities for national banks) are tracked in full, including verification of which letters are currently operative, in the Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillar; this pillar cross-references rather than duplicates that research.

GENIUS Act reserve, attestation, and interest-prohibition mechanics, and the full permitted-payment-stablecoin-issuer regulator allocation (including the Federal Reserve's role for state-member-bank-subsidiary issuers), are tracked in full in the Stablecoins pillar; this pillar cross-references rather than duplicates that research.

The Federal Register documents for the Section 9(13) policy statement (Doc. 2025-23548) and the payment-account proposal (Doc. 2026-10375) could not be fetched directly by this research (access-control page returned in place of document text); their substance is corroborated here against the Federal Reserve Board's own press releases (which were fetched directly) and multiple independent secondary legal-analysis sources, not against the Federal Register document text itself.

The Federal Reserve's proposed 'payment account' (Federal Register Doc. 2026-10375, published 26 May 2026) is a proposal only, with a comment period closing approximately 25 July 2026, after this pillar state's generation date; no final rule exists, and this pillar makes no prediction about whether or in what form one will issue.

The only currently binding federal constraint on a US central bank digital currency is Executive Order 14178 (23 January 2025), an executive action; H.R. 1919 (Anti-CBDC Surveillance State Act), which would make a materially similar prohibition statutory, passed the House on 17 July 2025 and had its Anti-CBDC provisions attached to a House amendment to S. 1318 recorded at the Senate desk on 29 April 2026, but no Senate agreement to that amendment had been identified as of the most recent sources located for this research; the bill is not enacted, and this pillar records only that status, with no prediction about the executive order's durability or the bill's prospects.

State banking charters, including special-purpose depository institution regimes (e.g., state trust-company and SPDI frameworks some crypto firms use as an alternative to a national charter), are real and operationally significant but are out of scope of this federal-level-only pillar.

The CLARITY Act (H.R. 3633) and the September 2025/March 2026 SEC-CFTC joint positions on spot crypto trading are tracked in the Exchanges (VATP) pillar, not here, notwithstanding some overlap with bank-affiliated trading venues; this pillar does not re-detail them.
